questions.question: What is the main purpose of fine-tuning a pretrained BERT model?
options: To adapt it to specific downstream tasks with less data and computation
To translate it to other languages
questions.explanation: Fine-tuning adapts the pretrained BERT to specific tasks by training only the task-specific layers, requiring less data and compute than training from scratch.

questions.question: Why is BERT called a bidirectional model?
options: It can translate between two languages
It considers both left and right context for each word
questions.explanation: BERT is bidirectional because the self-attention mechanism allows each word to attend to all other words, incorporating both left and right context simultaneously.

questions.question: What is masked self-attention used for in the Transformer decoder?
options: To prevent the model from attending to future tokens during generation
questions.explanation: Masked self-attention prevents the decoder from looking at future tokens during training, ensuring the model learns to generate text left-to-right autoregressively.

questions.question: What problem does the attention mechanism solve that RNNs struggle with?
options: Slow training due to sequential processing
Difficulty capturing long-range dependencies in text
questions.explanation: RNNs process text sequentially and struggle with long-range dependencies. Attention allows direct connections between any positions, solving this limitation.

questions.question: What property do word embeddings capture about words?
questions.explanation: Embeddings are vector representations that capture semantic meaning, allowing similar words to have similar vectors and enabling mathematical operations on meaning.

questions.question: What does zero-shot learning mean in the context of LLMs?
options: The model can perform tasks without any task-specific training examples
The model only works with zeros
questions.explanation: Zero-shot learning means the model can perform a task given only a natural language description, without seeing any examples of that task during training.

questions.question: How does masked language modeling work?
options: It masks random tokens and trains the model to predict them
It removes all vowels from text
questions.explanation: Masked language modeling randomly masks some tokens in the input and trains the model to predict the original tokens based on surrounding context.

questions.question: What mathematical relationship can be observed with well-trained word embeddings?
options: Words with similar meanings cluster together in vector space
All vectors must sum to one
Words with similar meanings have vectors that are far apart
questions.explanation: In well-trained embeddings, words with similar meanings cluster together in vector space, enabling operations like finding similar words through nearest neighbor search.

questions.question: Which special token does BERT use to separate two sentences in a pair?
questions.explanation: The [SEP] token is used to separate two sentences when BERT processes sentence pairs, such as for next sentence prediction or sentence similarity tasks.

questions.question: What does multi-head attention allow the model to do?
options: Attend to different representation subspaces at different positions
questions.explanation: Multi-head attention uses multiple attention heads to capture different types of relationships and attend to different representation subspaces simultaneously.

questions.question: What is the primary capability of generative models like GPT?
options: Generating coherent text by predicting the next token
questions.explanation: Generative models predict the next token given previous tokens, enabling them to generate coherent and contextually appropriate text autoregressively.

questions.question: What is the special [CLS] token used for in BERT?
options: As a classification token whose embedding represents the entire input
To mark the end of text
questions.explanation: The [CLS] token is added at the beginning of input, and its final hidden state serves as an aggregate representation for classification tasks.

questions.question: How does self-attention differ from traditional encoder-decoder attention?
options: It allows each position to attend to all positions in the same sequence
It only attends to the previous word
It only works with short sentences
questions.explanation: Self-attention allows every position in a sequence to attend to every other position in that same sequence, capturing relationships regardless of distance.

questions.question: What is the first step in creating a bag-of-words representation?
options: Tokenization - splitting text into individual units
questions.explanation: Tokenization is the process of splitting text into individual units (tokens), which is the essential first step before creating bag-of-words representations.

questions.question: What was ChatGPT initially powered by?
questions.explanation: ChatGPT was initially powered by GPT-3.5, a variant of the GPT-3 architecture that was fine-tuned for conversational interactions.

questions.question: What makes 'large' language models 'large'?
options: They only work with long documents
They have billions of parameters and are trained on massive datasets
questions.explanation: The 'large' in LLM refers to model scale: billions to hundreds of billions of parameters, trained on massive text corpora, enabling emergent capabilities.

questions.question: What was the key innovation introduced in the 'Attention Is All You Need' paper?
options: Recurrent neural networks with more layers
The Transformer architecture based solely on attention mechanisms
questions.explanation: The Transformer architecture eliminated recurrence entirely, using only attention mechanisms, which enabled parallel training and better handling of long-range dependencies.

questions.question: What type of tasks are encoder-only models like BERT primarily designed for?
questions.explanation: Encoder-only models excel at creating contextualized representations and are primarily used for classification, embedding extraction, and understanding tasks.

questions.question: What was the parameter count of GPT-3 compared to GPT-2?
options: 175 billion parameters, much larger than GPT-2's 1.5 billion
questions.explanation: GPT-3 dramatically scaled to 175 billion parameters, about 100 times larger than GPT-2, enabling few-shot and zero-shot learning capabilities.

questions.question: What is the primary limitation of bag-of-words models in natural language processing?
options: They can only work with English text
They ignore word order and semantic relationships between words
They require too much computational power
questions.explanation: Bag-of-words models treat text as an unordered collection of words, losing information about word order and failing to capture semantic relationships between words.

questions.question: What is the difference between representation models and generative models?
options: Representation models create embeddings; generative models produce text
questions.explanation: Representation models (like BERT) focus on creating contextual embeddings for understanding tasks, while generative models (like GPT) focus on producing text.

questions.question: In the original Transformer, what components are stacked to create the architecture?
options: Encoder and/or decoder blocks with attention and feedforward layers
questions.explanation: The Transformer stacks multiple identical encoder and/or decoder blocks, each containing self-attention and feedforward sublayers with residual connections.

questions.question: What does it mean that generative models are 'completion models'?
options: They continue or complete text given a prompt
questions.explanation: Generative models are called completion models because they take a prompt and generate a continuation or completion of that text.

questions.question: Why might someone use an API to access LLMs instead of running locally?
options: Large models require significant GPU resources that may not be locally available
questions.explanation: Large language models require substantial GPU memory and compute, making APIs practical when local hardware is insufficient or for easier deployment.

questions.question: What does the Hugging Face Transformers library provide?
options: Pre-trained models, tokenizers, and tools for working with language models
questions.explanation: Hugging Face Transformers provides access to thousands of pretrained models, tokenizers, training tools, and pipelines for NLP tasks.

questions.question: What type of architecture do GPT models use?
questions.explanation: GPT models use a decoder-only architecture, stacking Transformer decoder blocks with masked self-attention for autoregressive text generation.

questions.question: What does 'feature extraction' mean when using BERT?
options: Using BERT's hidden states as input features for other models
questions.explanation: Feature extraction uses BERT's contextualized hidden states as rich input features for other machine learning models without modifying BERT's weights.

questions.question: What type of architecture does BERT use?
questions.explanation: BERT uses an encoder-only architecture, stacking multiple Transformer encoder blocks without any decoder components.

questions.question: What training task does BERT use to learn language representations?
options: Masked language modeling and next sentence prediction
questions.explanation: BERT is trained on masked language modeling (predicting masked words) and next sentence prediction to learn bidirectional contextual representations.

questions.question: What is the role of a tokenizer when using a language model?
options: To convert text into tokens that the model can process
questions.explanation: Tokenizers convert raw text into the token IDs that models understand, handling tasks like splitting words, handling special characters, and adding special tokens.

questions.question: What is a key advantage of few-shot learning with LLMs?
options: It can perform tasks with just a few examples in the prompt
questions.explanation: Few-shot learning allows large language models to adapt to new tasks by providing just a few examples in the prompt, without any gradient updates.

questions.question: Why can't decoder-only models like GPT create embeddings like BERT?
options: They are optimized for generation, not representation
questions.explanation: Decoder-only models are architecturally optimized for autoregressive generation rather than creating rich contextual embeddings for understanding tasks.

questions.question: How do GPT models generate text?
options: Autoregressively, one token at a time from left to right
questions.explanation: GPT generates text autoregressively: it predicts one token, adds it to the sequence, and uses the extended sequence to predict the next token.

questions.question: What is the benefit of using a pipeline in the Transformers library?
options: It simplifies using models by combining tokenization, model inference, and output processing
questions.explanation: The pipeline abstraction simplifies model usage by automatically handling tokenization, model inference, and output post-processing in one call.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the feedforward network in a Transformer block?
options: To process each position's representation independently after attention
questions.explanation: After the attention mechanism creates contextualized representations, the feedforward network processes each position independently to further transform these representations.

questions.question: What emerged as the 'Year of Generative AI'?
questions.explanation: 2022-2023 marked the explosion of generative AI into mainstream use with ChatGPT's release and rapid adoption across industries.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the transformers.pipeline() function?
options: To convert models to different formats
To provide a simple interface for common NLP tasks
questions.explanation: The pipeline() function provides a high-level API that handles tokenization, model inference, and output formatting for common NLP tasks like text generation and classification.

questions.question: In word2vec, what happens to the embeddings of two words that frequently appear in similar contexts?
options: Their embeddings become closer together in vector space
Their embeddings are normalized to zero
They become orthogonal to each other
questions.explanation: Word2vec learns that words appearing in similar contexts have similar meanings, so their vector embeddings are positioned closer together in the embedding space.

questions.question: What is a key advantage of using pretrained models like BERT?
options: They leverage knowledge from large pretraining corpora, reducing task-specific data needs
questions.explanation: Pretrained models capture general language understanding from large corpora, allowing them to perform well on downstream tasks with less labeled data.

questions.question: What is a key characteristic of autoregressive text generation?
options: Each token depends on previously generated tokens
Tokens are generated in random order
questions.explanation: Autoregressive generation means each token is generated conditioned on all previously generated tokens, creating a left-to-right dependency chain.

questions.question: How does word2vec initialize word embeddings before training?
questions.explanation: Word2vec initializes embeddings with random values and then updates them during training based on the neural network's learning of word co-occurrence patterns.

questions.question: What was a major factor in GPT-2's improved performance over GPT-1?
options: Larger model size (1.5 billion parameters) and more training data
questions.explanation: GPT-2 scaled up significantly with 1.5 billion parameters and was trained on a larger and more diverse dataset, leading to substantially better performance.

questions.question: What is the key advantage of dense vector embeddings over sparse bag-of-words representations?
options: They require less storage space only
They capture semantic meaning and enable similarity measurement
questions.explanation: Dense embeddings capture semantic meaning, allowing measurement of word similarity through vector distance calculations, which bag-of-words cannot do.

questions.question: What does the context window determine in word embedding models?
options: The size of the embedding vector
Which neighboring words are considered for learning embeddings
questions.explanation: The context window defines how many neighboring words (before and after) are considered when learning word representations based on co-occurrence.

questions.question: What does the encoder in an encoder-decoder architecture create?
options: A context embedding representing the entire input sequence
questions.explanation: The encoder processes the input sequence and generates a context embedding that represents the meaning of the entire input for the decoder to use.

questions.question: Why was the Transformer architecture a breakthrough compared to RNNs?
options: It could be trained in parallel and handled long-range dependencies better
questions.explanation: Transformers eliminate sequential processing, enabling parallel training on modern hardware and better capturing of long-range dependencies through direct attention connections.

questions.question: What is the role of positional embeddings in Transformers?
options: To provide information about token position since attention is order-agnostic
questions.explanation: Since attention mechanisms process all positions simultaneously without inherent order information, positional embeddings add position information to each token.

questions.question: What technique did word2vec use to create semantic word representations?
options: Neural networks trained to predict word context
questions.explanation: Word2vec uses neural networks trained on large text corpora to predict word contexts, learning dense vector embeddings that capture semantic meaning based on word co-occurrence patterns.

questions.question: Which type of embedding is created at the document level by bag-of-words?
questions.explanation: Bag-of-words creates embeddings at the document level by representing the entire document as a vector of word frequencies, not individual word embeddings.

questions.question: What is the two-step process for adapting foundation models?
options: Pretraining on large corpora, then fine-tuning on specific tasks
questions.explanation: Foundation models are first pretrained on large unlabeled corpora to learn general language understanding, then fine-tuned on specific tasks with labeled data.
